params.text:
Part of a bigger duty

Whistleblowing does not stand alone. It sits alongside two duties you already hold:

  • Safeguarding — protecting adults at risk from abuse and neglect. Many whistleblowing concerns are also safeguarding concerns and must be reported straight away.
  • Duty of candour — being open and honest when something goes wrong, including telling the person affected and apologising. A culture of candour makes it easier to speak up before harm becomes serious.

The Freedom to Speak Up movement grew from failures where staff had raised concerns but were ignored. Its aim is a culture where speaking up is normal, welcomed and safe.

If in doubt, speak up

Fear, loyalty to colleagues, or thinking “it's not my place” can all get in the way. But the cost of staying silent is measured in the safety of the people we support. You do not need all the answers — you only need to report what you have seen to someone who can act. Speaking up is not disloyalty; it is exactly what good care looks like.

params.question: You should wait until you have gathered full proof before raising a whistleblowing concern.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. You do not need full proof, only a reasonable belief. Report what you have seen promptly and let it be investigated.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. You only need a reasonable belief that something is wrong. Waiting to gather proof can leave people at risk — if in doubt, speak up.
